description:
You are helping a script-developer prepare a little utilities area under their home directory.  
Perform every step from a normal, non-privileged user shell (no sudo).

Target file-system layout and permissions that **must be reached**

1. Create the directory “/home/user/project_utils/secure_scripts”.  
   • After you finish, “/home/user/project_utils/secure_scripts” must exist and be readable by others but writable/executable only by its owner.  
   • Numerical permission mode that the automated checker will look for: 750 (rwx r-x –––).

2. Inside that directory create a script stub called “backup.sh” with exactly this single line of content  
   #!/bin/bash  
   echo "Backup script stub"  
   (include the newline at the end of the second line).  
   • The file must be plain text, not executable by group/other, and not readable by “other”.  
   • Numerical permission mode that the checker expects: 740 (rwx r-- –––).

3. Produce a verification log “/home/user/perm_check.log” containing **exactly three lines**, no extra spaces or blank lines.  
   The lines must be written in the precise order shown below and must be the *verbatim* outputs of the commands, not the commands themselves.

   Line 1: The output of  
           stat -c "%a %n" /home/user/project_utils/secure_scripts

   Line 2: The output of  
           stat -c "%a %n" /home/user/project_utils/secure_scripts/backup.sh

   Line 3: The output of  
           ls -l /home/user/project_utils/secure_scripts/backup.sh | awk '{print $1" "$9}'

   Because the checker will parse the file literally, be sure that:  
   • Each line ends with a single newline.  
   • No surrounding quotation marks are added.  
   • Numerical modes appear exactly as produced by stat (e.g., “750 …” and “740 …”).

Hints (do NOT output these lines verbatim):
• Remember to create parent directories if they don’t exist.  
• Use chmod to set the numeric modes.  
• You can verify your work interactively with “stat” and “ls -l” before writing to perm_check.log, but only the log file will be graded.

End state summary the grader will look for
• /home/user/project_utils/secure_scripts     → mode 750  
• /home/user/project_utils/secure_scripts/backup.sh → mode 740 and contains the two-line stub exactly.  
• /home/user/perm_check.log exists with the three lines of command output described above.

The entire task can be accomplished with roughly three or four shell commands plus log creation.
truth:
Expected final permission bits:
1. Directory /home/user/project_utils/secure_scripts → 750
2. File /home/user/project_utils/secure_scripts/backup.sh → 740

Expected exact contents of /home/user/project_utils/secure_scripts/backup.sh (including trailing newline on second line):
#!/bin/bash
echo "Backup script stub"

Expected pattern inside /home/user/perm_check.log (all paths literal):
Line 1 must start with “750 /home/user/project_utils/secure_scripts”
Line 2 must start with “740 /home/user/project_utils/secure_scripts/backup.sh”
Line 3 must look like “-rwxr-----[ ]/home/user/project_utils/secure_scripts/backup.sh”
(The first field will be “-rwxr-----” followed by a single space and the full path.)

The grader will:
1. stat -c "%a" on both paths to confirm 750 and 740.
2. diff the backup.sh content against the two-line stub above.
3. Read /home/user/perm_check.log and assert the three-line format and numeric values/prefixes specified.
